thm:welfare-identity.proof_tex: Fix a deterministic policy pi in Pi. By def:welfare-regret, V_P(pi_star)-V_P(pi)=E_P[(pi_star(X)-pi(X)) tau_P(X)]. The bounded-outcome assumption gives mu_a in [-1,1], hence tau_P in [-2,2], so all displayed expectations are finite. For each x, if tau_P(x)>0 then pi_star(x)=1 and (pi_star(x)-pi(x)) tau_P(x) equals tau_P(x) when pi(x)=0 and equals 0 when pi(x)=1. This is exactly |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. If tau_P(x)<0 then pi_star(x)=0 and (pi_star(x)-pi(x)) tau_P(x) equals -tau_P(x) when pi(x)=1 and equals 0 when pi(x)=0, again equal to |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. If tau_P(x)=0, both sides are zero, regardless of the tie convention in pi_star. Therefore (pi_star-pi) tau_P=|tau_P| 1{pi != pi_star} pointwise P_X-a.s. Taking expectation over X gives R_P(pi)=E_P[|tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)}]=integral |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x). The iid sampling assumption is not needed for this population identity.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:iid and ass:bounded-outcome, for every deterministic pi in Pi, R_P(pi) = E_P[ |tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)} ] = integral over X of |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x).

defs.def:welfare-regret: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

thm:margin-localization.proof_tex: Let D_pi={x: pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. By thm:welfare-identity, R_P(pi)=integral_{D_pi} |tau_P(x)| dP_X(x). First suppose R_P(pi)=0. Then |tau_P| 1_{D_pi}=0 P_X-a.s. Hence D_pi can have positive mass only inside {tau_P=0}. Under ass:zero-effect either P_X{tau_P=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-a.s. on {tau_P=0}; in both cases P_X(D_pi)=0, so the claimed inequality holds. Now suppose R=R_P(pi)>0. For any u in (0,u_0], decompose D_pi into the zero-effect set, the small-margin set, and the set {|tau_P|>u}. The zero-effect part has P_X-mass zero by ass:zero-effect. The margin assumption gives P_X(0<|tau_P(X)|<=u)<=C_m u^alpha. On {|tau_P|>u}, the welfare identity gives u P_X(D_pi cap {|tau_P|>u}) <= integral_{D_pi} |tau_P| dP_X=R. Thus P_X(D_pi)<=C_m u^alpha+R/u for every u in (0,u_0]. If R^{1/(1+alpha)}<=u_0, choose u=R^{1/(1+alpha)} and get P_X(D_pi)<=(C_m+1) R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. If R^{1/(1+alpha)}>u_0, then P_X(D_pi)<=1<=u_0^{-alpha} R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. Taking C=max{C_m+1,u_0^{-alpha}} proves P_X(D_pi)<=C R_P(pi)^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. For alpha=0 the same display reads P_X(D_pi)<=C and is immediate from the preceding bound with exponent zero.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin and ass:zero-effect there is C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that for every pi in Pi, P_X(D_pi) <= C R_P(pi)^(alpha/(1+alpha)).

assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:zero-effect: Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.

prop:overlap-envelope.proof_tex: Let gamma>0, h in (0,1), beta>=0, v=h^beta, and u=h^{beta/gamma}. Then u^alpha v^{1/gamma}=(h^{beta/gamma})^alpha (h^beta)^{1/gamma}=h^{(alpha+1) beta/gamma}. Since h lies in (0,1), h^a >= h^alpha holds if and only if a<=alpha. Applying this with a=(alpha+1) beta/gamma gives u^alpha v^{1/gamma}>=h^alpha if and only if beta<=alpha gamma/(alpha+1). By def:exponents this endpoint is beta_ag, and equality holds when beta=beta_ag. For an active block with mass proportional to h^alpha, contrast h, and propensity h^beta, the least favorable overlap check is the smallest admissible window that both includes the weak-arm propensity layer and satisfies v<=c_o u^gamma. Up to constants this window is v=h^beta and u=h^{beta/gamma}. The overlap envelope permits the block precisely when its mass h^alpha is no larger than the right side u^alpha v^{1/gamma}, which is equivalent to beta<=beta_ag. Larger beta means smaller propensity h^beta and therefore less information; beta_ag is the largest admissible, hence least informative, weak-arm exponent.

snapshot.stmt: For alpha >= 0, gamma > 0, h in (0,1), beta >= 0, at the tight window v=h^beta, u=h^(beta/gamma): u^alpha v^(1/gamma) = h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma), and u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= h^alpha iff beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1), with equality at beta = beta_ag. Hence a block of mass ~ h^alpha, contrast ~ h, propensity ~ h^beta meets the ass:overlap-decay envelope iff beta <= beta_ag, and beta_ag is the least informative admissible weak-arm exponent.

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

lem:witness-membership.proof_tex: Fix sigma in {+,-}. The witness has X uniform on [0,1], active block B_n of mass m_n=c_B h_n^alpha, propensity e=q_n on B_n and e=1/2 off B_n. For all large n, h_n in (0,u_0) and q_n in (0,1/2], so ass:positivity holds. Choose the off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0,2), possible because ass:margin-window states u_0<2 and bounded outcomes allow contrasts in [-2,2]. Define potential-outcome conditional distributions equal to the displayed treatment-arm outcome laws and independent of A conditional on X; then consistency and unconfoundedness hold by construction. The outcomes take values in [-1,1], so ass:bounded-outcome holds. The contrast is sigma h_n on B_n and tau_0 off B_n, hence it is never zero; ass:zero-effect holds. For the margin condition, if 0<u<h_n the event {0<|tau|<=u} is empty, while if h_n<=u<=u_0 the event is exactly B_n and has mass c_B h_n^alpha<=c_B u^alpha. Choosing c_B<=C_m gives ass:margin. For the joint overlap-decay condition when gamma>0, off B_n is excluded because tau_0>u_0. If u<h_n or v<q_n the event is empty. Otherwise the event is B_n, but the constraint v<=c_o u^gamma and v>=q_n force u>=(q_n/c_o)^{1/gamma}. Thus u^alpha v^{1/gamma}>=c_o^{-alpha/gamma} q_n^{(alpha+1)/gamma}. At beta_ag=alpha gamma/(alpha+1), q_n=h_n^{beta_ag} when beta_ag>0, and q_n^{(alpha+1)/gamma}=h_n^alpha. Taking c_B small enough relative to C_o and c_o yields c_B h_n^alpha<=C_o u^alpha v^{1/gamma}. If beta_ag=0 the active propensity is the fixed constant 1/4; for v<1/4 the event is empty and for v>=1/4 the same margin bound c_B h_n^alpha<=C_o u^alpha applies after enlarging constants. For gamma=0, the convention v^{1/gamma}=1 gives the same verification: the event is empty unless u>=h_n and v>=q_n=1/4, and then its mass c_B h_n^alpha is at most C_o u^alpha. The enlarged law class also requires marginal overlap. For gamma>0, P{p_P(X)<=v}=0 when v<q_n, is m_n when q_n<=v<1/2, and is at most 1 for v>=1/2. At v=q_n the required right side is C_om q_n^{1/gamma}; because q_n=h_n^{beta_ag} gives q_n^{1/gamma}=h_n^{alpha/(alpha+1)} and h_n^alpha<=h_n^{alpha/(alpha+1)} for alpha>=0 up to the constant case alpha=0, a sufficiently large C_om verifies the bound; larger v only increases the right side, and v>=1/2 is handled by increasing C_om. For gamma=0, strict overlap holds with underline_p=1/4 since p_P is 1/4 on B_n and 1/2 off B_n, and the marginal-overlap convention is trivial after enlarging C_om. The policy pi_star is x -> 1 under P_{n,+} and x -> 1{x notin B_n} under P_{n,-}. If these two policies belong to Pi, then ass:optimal-in-class holds for the witnesses; ass:policy-class is a standing property of Pi. Therefore, for all large n, the two witnesses satisfy every member condition in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma).

snapshot.stmt: For all large n the corrected witness laws P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, and ass:overlap-decay. If, in addition, the two explicit witness-optimal policies x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n} belong to Pi, then P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} lie in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma). (Normalization: the margin window satisfies u_0<2, so an off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0, 2) exists under bounded outcomes |Y|<=1.)

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:unconfoundedness: (Y(0), Y(1)) is independent of A given X under P.

lem:two-point-divergence.proof_tex: The two witness laws have the same X marginal, the same propensity, and the same conditional outcome law except on the cell C_n={X in B_n,A=1}. The P_{n,-}-probability of C_n is m_n q_n, where m_n=P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. Conditional on C_n, the outcome is Bernoulli with success probabilities p_+=(1+h_n)/2 and p_-=(1-h_n)/2 under the plus and minus laws. For h_n<=1/2, the Bernoulli chi-square is (p_+-p_-)^2/p_- + ((1-p_+)-(1-p_-))^2/(1-p_-) = h_n^2((1-p_-)^{-1}+p_-^{-1}) <= 8 h_n^2. Since the laws agree off C_n, the full one-observation chi-square is m_n q_n times this conditional chi-square, hence chi^2(P_{n,+}||P_{n,-})<=C m_n q_n h_n^2. By the definitions m_n is proportional to h_n^alpha and q_n is either a fixed constant when beta_ag=0 or h_n^{beta_ag}; in both cases this is bounded by C h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}. With h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, the one-observation chi-square is at most C/n. Product chi-square tensorizes as 1+chi^2(P_+^{n}||P_-^{n})=(1+chi^2(P_+||P_-))^n. Therefore 1+chi^2(P_{n,+}^{n}||P_{n,-}^{n}) <= (1+C/n)^n <= exp(C), so the n-fold chi-square is bounded by a constant independent of n.

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, the per-observation chi-square is chi^2(P_{n,+} || P_{n,-}) <= C m_n q_n h_n^2 with m_n=P_X(B_n)~h_n^alpha and q_n~h_n^{beta_ag}, hence ~ h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}; for h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)} the n-fold divergence is bounded, chi^2(P_{n,+}^{⊗n} || P_{n,-}^{⊗n}) <= C (equivalently 1+chi^2(P^{⊗n}) = (1+O(h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}))^n <= e^{C}).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:regret-separation.proof_tex: On B_n the plus witness has tau=+h_n, so its optimal policy is 1; the minus witness has tau=-h_n, so its optimal policy is 0. Off B_n both witnesses have the same positive contrast tau_0 and the same optimal label 1. By thm:welfare-identity, for any deterministic policy pi, R_{P_{n,+}}(pi) contributes h_n P_X(B_n cap {pi=0}) on B_n, while R_{P_{n,-}}(pi) contributes h_n P_X(B_n cap {pi=1}) on B_n. These two subsets partition B_n because pi is binary. Hence R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(pi) >= h_n P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^{1+alpha}. Consequently max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= (c_B/2) h_n^{1+alpha}. Taking the infimum over pi in Pi gives the claimed separation.

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, inf_{pi in Pi} max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= c h_n^(1+alpha): the optimal policies are opposite on B_n, so any pi misclassifies B_n under at least one law and pays regret >= (1/2) P_X(B_n) h_n ~ h_n^(1+alpha).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.

lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq.proof_tex: This is the classical two-point testing inequality. Tsybakov, Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation, 2009, Chapter 2, Theorems 2.2 and 2.6, gives Le Cam's method and the Bretagnolle-Huber bound: for any test psi between product laws P_+^n and P_-^n, P_+^n(psi != +)+P_-^n(psi != -) >= (1/2) exp(-KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)). Since KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)<=log(1+chi^2(P_+^n||P_-^n)) and 1+chi^2(P_+^n||P_-^n)=(1+chi^2(P_+||P_-))^n, the assumption chi^2(P_+||P_-)<=c_0/n implies KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)<=c_0. Thus every test has summed error at least c_1=(1/2) exp(-c_0). The standard loss-separation form of Le Cam's method in the same references then yields a minimax lower bound equal to the separation radius times this positive testing-error constant, up to the harmless factor used to pass from summed to maximal risk.

snapshot.stmt: Le Cam two-point testing bound. For product laws P_+^{\otimes n}, P_-^{\otimes n} with per-observation chi-square chi^2(P_+ || P_-) <= c_0/n, every test psi satisfies inf_psi [P_+^{\otimes n}(psi != +) + P_-^{\otimes n}(psi != -)] >= c_1 > 0 for a constant c_1=c_1(c_0); combined with a parameter separation this yields the two-point minimax lower bound.

thm:minimax-lower.proof_tex: By lem:witness-membership, under the stated richness assumption on Pi the two laws P_{n,+} and P_{n,-} are elements of P_class(alpha,gamma) for all large n. Let delta_n=(c_B/2) h_n^{1+alpha}, the separation from lem:regret-separation. Fix any measurable Pi-valued estimator hat pi. From its output define a test psi by psi=+ if R_{P_{n,+}}(hat pi)<=R_{P_{n,-}}(hat pi), and psi=- otherwise. For every deterministic output pi, if psi(pi)=- then R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)>delta_n, because R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(pi)>=2 delta_n; similarly, if psi(pi)=+ then R_{P_{n,-}}(pi)>=delta_n. Therefore E_{P_{n,+}} R_{P_{n,+}}(hat pi)+E_{P_{n,-}} R_{P_{n,-}}(hat pi) >= delta_n [P_{n,+}^n(psi=-)+P_{n,-}^n(psi=+)]. Lem:two-point-divergence gives chi^2(P_{n,+}||P_{n,-})<=c_0/n for a constant c_0, and lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq gives the bracketed testing error at least c_1>0. Hence max_sigma E_{P_{n,sigma}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(hat pi) >= (c_1/2) delta_n. Because the witnesses lie in P_class, sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(hat pi) is at least this quantity. Taking the infimum over all estimators yields M_n(alpha,gamma)>=c h_n^{1+alpha}. Finally, h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, so h_n^{1+alpha}=n^{-(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}=n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} by def:exponents.

snapshot.stmt: Assume the two explicit witness-optimal policies of the corrected def:two-point-witness, namely x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n}, belong to Pi for all large n. Then M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, with c > 0 independent of n.

defs.def:minimax-regret: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf over measurable data-dependent Pi-valued estimators pi_hat of sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

oeq:feasible-upper.proof_tex: The construction is the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM in def:feasible-erm with analysis clip q_n=n^{-s_n} for gamma>0, where s_n is chosen to maximize the balance phi(s)=min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} up to an immaterial logarithmic grid error. At gamma=0 choose any fixed q<=underline_p. The exact drift identity is lem:clip-bias. Lem:clipped-score-drift-bound converts it into the honest global additive drift B_q<=C(r_mu,n r_e,n/q+r_mu,n q^{1/(2 gamma)}) for gamma>0, and B_q<=C r_mu,n r_e,n under strict overlap. Lem:clipped-score-variance-envelope gives the localized conditional variance scale q^{-1} R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. Combining this variance localization with ass:vc-localized-envelope and the ERM basic inequality gives the master bound in lem:feasible-master-bound. Balancing q=n^{-s} in that bound yields lem:clip-balance-exponent: for gamma>0 the best certified exponent of the plain feasible clipped-AIPW ERM is g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}, and the reported feasible exponent is r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),g(a,c,gamma)}. Thus sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(hat pi_n) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p. The inequality r_feas<=r_star is immediate from the definition. At gamma=0, beta_ag=0, r_star=A_alpha, the clip is fixed, and the formula reduces to r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}; in particular it equals r_star exactly when the product nuisance exponent c is at least A_alpha.

snapshot.stmt: CONSTRUCT-AND-DETERMINE. Assume gamma<=alpha+1, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:vc-localized-envelope, and at gamma=0 also ass:strict-overlap-endpoint. For the plain cross-fit clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with clip q chosen by the analysis, DERIVE (do NOT pre-specify) the best worst-case welfare-regret exponent r_feas with sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C n^{-r_feas} (log n)^p, by balancing the localized variance against the estimated-nuisance CLIP BIAS over q. State the clip q and the resulting r_feas in closed form, confirm r_feas<=r_star, and reduce at gamma=0.

defs.def:feasible-erm: Vhat_{n,q}(pi) = n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}) with K-fold cross-fitted nuisances etahat and clip q in (0,1/2]; pi_hat_n in argmax_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi). The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter (chosen by the bias-variance balance).
defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).
defs.def:feasible-rate: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c with r_mu_n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu_n r_e_n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2: the clipped-AIPW ERM welfare regret is bounded by the GLOBAL (additive, NON-localized) estimated-nuisance drift r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2 gamma)} plus a localized stochastic term whose radius uses the AIPW conditional VARIANCE Var(Gamma_q|X) <~ 1/max{p,q} (scale n^{-1/2} q^{-1/2}), NOT the crude envelope q^{-1}. Balancing the clip q=n^{-s} gives g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma), g(a,c,gamma)}. At gamma=0 (strict-overlap floor, q fixed) r_feas=min{A_alpha, c}, which equals r_star=A_alpha when c>=A_alpha. The drift is GLOBAL because ||b_q||_2 cannot carry the r_mu r_e product from L2 bounds on each factor; only E|b_q| (Cauchy-Schwarz, L1) gives the product, and that enters regret additively, not through the margin self-bound.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:marginal-overlap: P{p_P(X) <= v} <= C_om v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o, with the convention v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:nuisance-rate: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the finite-VC class Pi there is a localized Rademacher/Dudley entropy-integral envelope psi with psi(r) <= C sqrt(d_Pi/n) r polylog(n) controlling the localized offset empirical processes uniformly over the class.
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].
assumptions.ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances: The cross-fitted outcome-regression estimates muhat_0, muhat_1 take values in [-1,1].
assumptions.ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents: There exist a >= 0, c >= 1/2 and constants C_mu, C_prod such that r_mu_n <= C_mu n^(-a) and r_mu_n r_e_n <= C_prod n^(-c) for all large n.

lem:clip-bias.proof_tex: Fix a measurable plug-in nuisance triple bar eta=(bar mu_0,bar mu_1,bar e), let bar e_q=min{1-q,max{q,bar e}}, and set Delta_a=bar mu_a-mu_a. For a fixed x, abbreviate e=e_P(x), mu_a=mu_a(x), bar mu_a=bar mu_a(x), and bar e_q=bar e_q(x). By def:clipped-aipw-score and the conditional-mean identities E[A|X=x]=e, E[Y|A=1,X=x]=mu_1, and E[Y|A=0,X=x]=mu_0, E[Gamma_q(O;bar eta)|X=x]=bar mu_1-bar mu_0+(e/bar e_q)(mu_1-bar mu_1)-((1-e)/(1-bar e_q))(mu_0-bar mu_0). Subtract tau_P(x)=mu_1-mu_0. The result is Delta_1(1-e/bar e_q)-Delta_0(1-(1-e)/(1-bar e_q)). Since 1-e/bar e_q=(bar e_q-e)/bar e_q and 1-(1-e)/(1-bar e_q)=-(bar e_q-e)/(1-bar e_q), the drift equals (bar e_q-e_P(x))(Delta_1(x)/bar e_q(x)+Delta_0(x)/(1-bar e_q(x))). This proves the displayed identity. It also shows the cancellation cases exactly: the prefactor vanishes when bar e_q=e_P, and the bracket vanishes when both regression errors vanish. The condition p_P(x)>q alone concerns only the true propensity and does not imply bar e_q=e_P, so no cancellation follows merely from being outside the true clipped region.

snapshot.stmt:
Exact clipped-score drift identity. For any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), with \(\bar e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\), the conditional mean drift satisfies
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q(x)-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
Hence cancellation holds only where \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\) or both regression errors vanish; it does not follow merely from \(p_P(x)>q\). Under the frozen assumptions alone, no additive clipped-region bound of the form asserted in the current lemma is derivable.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

thm:rate-characterization.proof_tex: Proof. Work under the stated hypotheses: ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, the standing feasible-estimator conditions used in oeq:feasible-upper, richness of Pi for the two witness-optimal policies, and gamma <= alpha+1. Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). By def:exponents, beta_ag=0 when gamma=0 and beta_ag=alpha gamma/(alpha+1) when gamma>0, D_ag=2+alpha+beta_ag, and r_star(alpha,gamma)=(1+alpha)/D_ag. For the lower bound, thm:minimax-lower applies under the richness hypothesis on Pi and gives, for all large n, M_n(alpha,gamma) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} with c>0 independent of n. This is the left side of the asserted bracket. For the upper bound, take the specific cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM pi_hat_n from def:feasible-erm, with the clip selected as in oeq:feasible-upper. Since M_n is the infimum over all measurable Pi-valued estimators, the risk of this one estimator is an admissible upper witness: M_n(alpha,gamma) <= sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n). The result oeq:feasible-upper bounds the right-hand side by C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p. When gamma>0, the same result identifies the best certified balancing exponent for this plain feasible clipped-AIPW ERM as g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}, and def:feasible-rate sets r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),g(a,c,gamma)}. When gamma=0, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint allows a fixed clip q<=underline_p, beta_ag=0, and def:exponents gives r_star(alpha,0)=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha)=A_alpha; oeq:feasible-upper and def:feasible-rate reduce the feasible exponent to r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}. Combining these displays proves the two-sided bracket. It remains only to check the phase statements. For gamma>0, r_feas=min{r_star,g}, so r_feas<=r_star. Equality holds if and only if min{r_star,g}=r_star, equivalently g>=r_star. If g<r_star then r_feas=g and the gap r_star-r_feas is strictly positive; conversely a strictly positive gap forces g<r_star. At gamma=0, the identities r_star=A_alpha and r_feas=min{A_alpha,c} give equality if and only if c>=A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha), and a strict gap if and only if c<A_alpha. Finally, in the equal-exponent branch, the preceding bracket reads c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-r_star}(log n)^p, so the minimax polynomial exponent is resolved. In the strict-gap branch, the bracket remains c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-g(a,c,gamma)}(log n)^p with g<r_star; deciding whether the true feasible rate equals the converse exponent or the slower feasible exponent is exactly the residual question recorded as oeq:feasible-tight.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, the standing feasible-estimator conditions used in oeq:feasible-upper, Pi rich (witness-optimal policies in Pi), and gamma<=alpha+1: c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p, with r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}) and r_feas=min{r_star,g(a,c,gamma)} for g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} when gamma>0, and r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}. Moreover r_feas<=r_star; equality holds exactly when g(a,c,gamma)>=r_star(alpha,gamma), and at gamma=0 this is the condition c>=A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). The strict positive gap occurs precisely when g(a,c,gamma)<r_star(alpha,gamma); feasible tightness is resolved in the equal-exponent branch and remains open in the strict-gap branch (oeq:feasible-tight).

defs.def:feasible-rate: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c with r_mu_n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu_n r_e_n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2: the clipped-AIPW ERM welfare regret is bounded by the GLOBAL (additive, NON-localized) estimated-nuisance drift r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2 gamma)} plus a localized stochastic term whose radius uses the AIPW conditional VARIANCE Var(Gamma_q|X) <~ 1/max{p,q} (scale n^{-1/2} q^{-1/2}), NOT the crude envelope q^{-1}. Balancing the clip q=n^{-s} gives g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma), g(a,c,gamma)}. At gamma=0 (strict-overlap floor, q fixed) r_feas=min{A_alpha, c}, which equals r_star=A_alpha when c>=A_alpha. The drift is GLOBAL because ||b_q||_2 cannot carry the r_mu r_e product from L2 bounds on each factor; only E|b_q| (Cauchy-Schwarz, L1) gives the product, and that enters regret additively, not through the margin self-bound.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

oeq:feasible-tight.proof_tex: If g(a,c,gamma)>=r_star(alpha,gamma), then r_feas=r_star and thm:rate-characterization gives c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-r_star}(log n)^p, resolving the minimax polynomial exponent up to logarithms. If g(a,c,gamma)<r_star(alpha,gamma), the strongest proved statement is the bracket c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-g(a,c,gamma)}(log n)^p; whether this bracket is tight remains open.

snapshot.stmt: OPEN: determine the welfare-regret rate attainable by a genuinely feasible estimator, in particular the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm, under weak overlap. Can any feasible estimator attain the converse exponent r_star(alpha,gamma), or must every feasible procedure be strictly slower because the weak-arm outcome regression must be learned on the clipped region?
